text: The patient is positioned in a flank position with the head visible. The right-sided tumor is located near the ribs and costal margin. Due to the patient's severe scoliosis, the working space is reduced. The surgeon marks the costal margin, iliac crest, and pararectal line. A Veress needle is inserted, and pneumoperitoneum is established with a pressure of ten, preparing for the nephrectomy procedure.

The surgeon marks the trochanter and positions the trocars for the nephrectomy procedure. A 10mm camera is used for enhanced vision quality, while 5mm instruments are typically employed. On the right side, the trocars are placed two finger breadths below the costal margin to accommodate the liver's position.

The surgeon positions the optical trocar and working ports in a parallel configuration, avoiding interference with the liver. An incision is made for the ten millimeter trocar, and the camera is inserted to visualize the fascia layers, including fat, muscle fascia, muscle, and peritoneum. The endoscopic view reveals the layers of fat as the surgeon progresses through the tissue.

The surgeon advances the trocar through the abdominal wall, observing the whitish fascia layer before reaching the muscle tissue. The liver edge is visible, indicating a safe entry into the peritoneum. The surgeon checks for any bowel or vessel injury upon entry.

The surgeon switches to a thirty-degree lens for better visualization and hands it to the assistant, Dr. Gaia. The camera view shows the rectus muscle and the epigastric vessels clearly. The surgeon emphasizes the importance of placing the trochanter at the junction between the rectus muscle and the lateral muscle to avoid injuring the epigastric vessels.

The surgeon positions a twelve millimeter trocar for hemolog placement, ensuring it is safely above the epigastric vessels. The trocar is inserted without placing the gas at the entry point, maintaining safety in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses large trocars for enhanced vision and positions them strategically. A lens and tweezers are introduced, and the surgeon employs a bipolar instrument, the Endo Seal, to dissect the triangular ligament of the liver. The liver is separated to expose a hilar tumor, visible in the surgical field. The surgeon uses the Endo Seal from the left hand for better angle and control, identifying the phrenic vessel during the dissection.

The surgeon aggressively mobilizes the angle of the liver to free it up for retraction, using a bipolar sealant to safely dissect without entering the pleura. The liver is lifted to expose the upper pole, ensuring visibility of the kidney. The phrenic vessel is visible near the crux of the diaphragm, and a five millimeter trocar is placed at the level of the xiphoid.

The surgeon places an accessory five millimeter trocar at the level of the xiphoid and maneuvers under the liver, having freed the triangular ligament. The duodenum, inferior vena cava (IVC), tumor, and colon are visible. The surgeon begins dissecting the colon to identify and isolate the urethra, noting the tumor's proximity to the urethra.

The surgeon navigates the surgical field, noting the duodenum and inferior vena cava (IVC) visible in the current view. Due to the patient's scoliosis, the trocars are positioned higher than usual, causing discomfort during the procedure. The surgeon aims to isolate the ureter carefully to avoid accidental cutting while dissecting the tumor.

The surgeon identifies the adrenal gland and the renal vein, noting the presence of a double vein branch. The surgical field includes the crura of the diaphragm, which is thin, and the surgeon avoids anchoring to prevent pneumothorax. Efforts are made to elevate the liver further for better exposure.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the colon, revealing the gonadal vein and identifying the ureter nearby. The mesenteric fat appears yellow, contrasting with the translucent fat of the retroperitoneum.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and gonadal vein, ensuring a clear plane of dissection. The trocar is adjusted due to the patient's scoliosis, providing better visibility and distance from the kidney. The surgical field is cleaned for improved visualization.

The surgeon identifies a double vein and the inferior vena cava (IVC) in the surgical field. The artery is also visible, appearing small with a possible branch nearby. The surgeon uses a ten or twelve millimeter trocar with five millimeter instruments, noting the instability due to the size mismatch.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and begins to free it from surrounding tissue. The artery is visible, and the surgeon plans to work lower to find the main branch for clamping. An assistant, Dr. Boissier, is preparing to scrub in to aid in elevating the kidney for better visualization.

The surgeon identifies the common branch of the renal artery, noting its thin appearance, and observes the tumor located nearby. The artery has been dissected, and the scoliosis of the spine is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the tumor adjacent to the urethra and vein, preparing to de-fat the tumor while maintaining the fat on top for anchoring purposes. The tumor is visually observed laying on the urethra and vein, with plans to enucleate the tumor over the vein. Consideration is given to clamping the vein to control potential bleeding during dissection.

The surgeon visually identifies the demarcation of the tumor and begins defatting the tumor, preparing for enucleation. The anatomical structures, including vessels and urethra, are clearly visible, necessitating enucleation to preserve these structures.

The surgeon prepares to address the vein and enucleation, considering the complexity of the anatomy. An assistant is called to help with a lateral trocar insertion. The surgeon uses an Enseal device for dissection, focusing on a visible artery in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses an Enseal device to gently dissect around a branch of the artery and a branch of the vein, which appear to be supplying the tumor. The surgeon considers whether to cut and clamp these branches for a clampless procedure, noting the potential risk that these vessels may also supply the ureter or other parts of the kidney.

The surgeon carefully dissects the artery, identifying branches that supply the tumor and the lower pole of the kidney. The vessels are clamped to prevent excessive parenchyma loss during enucleation. The surgical field is prepared for further dissection, with consideration given to using ICG for better visualization of vessel supply.

The surgeon marks the dissection margin by pushing in and observing the tumor moving towards them, indicating the preferred margin for enucleation. The tumor's position is visually assessed without the use of ultrasound.

The surgeon marks the area for dissection and uses tweezers to clean the optical lens. The renal pelvis is visible, with the tumor located very close to it. The surgeon identifies the ureter's location and examines an artery branch that extends over and behind the tumor.

The surgeon carefully dissects the veins, considering options to clamp them if necessary. The camera moves closer to provide a detailed view of the surgical field, where the surgeon evaluates whether to clamp specific veins or directly clamp the artery. Assistance is requested for suction to maintain a clear view of the area.

The surgeon applies a Bulldog clamp to the artery using a Scanlan instrument, preparing to manage potential reflux or vein injury. The tumor is visible in the surgical field as the surgeon plans to address the vessels first.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers the scissors with the tip down to avoid penetrating the tumor capsule, visible in the surgical field. A small vein is identified, causing bleeding, which is managed by either using bipolar cautery or avoiding it. The surgeon adjusts the direction of the scissors as the tumor's orientation changes, highlighting the proximity of a vein that requires careful attention.

The surgeon identifies the artery and prepares to apply a Hem-o-lok clip to secure it. The vein is visible but not actively bleeding, and the decision is made to leave it unclamped for now, with plans to suture it after completing the dissection.

The surgeon navigates around the renal capsule, identifying a vessel entering the tumor, indicating it cannot be preserved. The surgical field shows the tumor in close proximity, requiring careful maneuvering. The surgeon prepares to clamp a vein to improve visibility, as the area is not bleeding significantly but visibility is compromised.

The surgeon uses a bulldog clamp to secure a major branch of a vessel near the tumor, avoiding entry into the tumor itself. A vein is identified and clamped, stopping the bleeding. The clip is placed on a calyx, marking the other side of the dissection area.

The surgeon carefully lifts the tumor, performing a partial nephrectomy while ensuring minimal cutting of the renal parenchyma. The tumor is successfully removed, and the surgeon inspects the margins to confirm complete enucleation without entering the tumor.

The surgeon uses suction to clear the surgical field, revealing an open vein that requires careful suturing to prevent bleeding. The absence of surrounding parenchyma presents a challenge for suturing. The camera is positioned between the surgeon's arms to optimize visualization during this critical step.

The surgeon places a separated suture to close the tissue, using a double hemlock to secure the suture in place. The technique involves approximating the tissue gradually, with the intention to tie everything at the end. The surgeon maneuvers the suture from left hand to right hand for better tension control.
The surgeon places another suture in the remaining parenchyma, connecting it to adjacent tissue. The area shows minimal bleeding, and the surgeon uses both hands to maneuver the suture placement.

The surgeon approximates and compresses an open vein using sutures, followed by the application of flow seal and surgicel to further compress the area. The monocryl suture is pulled to achieve better approximation, and a hemlock clip is placed for reinforcement.
The surgeon de-clamps the vein followed by the artery, observing the color of the kidney, which appears healthy with no immediate signs of bleeding.
The surgeon cuts the sutures and places the tumor in a bag. The retroperitoneal space is addressed to allow for additional compression on the kidney. A small artery is preserved during the procedure.
